reasoning: Exact_match_multi on ['artist','duration'] was far too restrictive (PC≈0.018, only 112 candidates), while token_blocking on 'name' with min_token_len=5 exploded to >2M candidates, far above the 350K limit. The title/artist fields are noisy and often formatted differently across Discogs and LastFM (e.g., artist name prefixed into the title on one side), making token or exact blocking brittle. Semantic_similarity over ['name','artist'] lets us capture fuzzy and structural variations while controlling candidate volume directly via top_k. With ~22.6K left rows and top_k=15, we expect about 339K candidate pairs (22,627 × 15), just under the 350K cap, while substantially increasing pair completeness toward the 0.95 target compared to exact blocking.

Previous exact and token-based blocking either missed most matches (PC=0.154) or generated far too many candidates. Release titles and artist names are short, well-structured text where semantic similarity is effective for fuzzy matching across Discogs and MusicBrainz. Using semantic_similarity on ['name','artist'] allows us to capture variations and minor differences in naming that exact/token blocking may miss. With ~22.6K left records and top_k=15, we get ~340K candidate pairs, which stays under the 350K limit while being much less restrictive than previous exact-match multi-column blocking, improving pair completeness.

Previous token-based blocking either produced too many candidates on name or too low pair completeness on artist alone. Exact matching on any single column is too brittle for these noisy title strings, and multi-column exact match is overly restrictive. The combined dataset size is modest (~15K rows), and semantic similarity with top_k≈20 yields ≈4,763×20 ≈95K candidate pairs, well under the 350K limit. Using both name and artist in the embedding captures semantic variation in titles while anchoring to the artist, which should materially improve recall toward PC ≥ 0.95 without exploding candidate volume.
